Thanks for the PR!

Apart from some minor details, this is looking good. To give you a bit more infos (without me giving a proper review):
- Import statements of different import types should only be separated by at most one empty line
- URL matcher regexes should not be defined as verbose if they aren't actually verbose
- Validation schemas should not be defined as class attributes, as they cause unnecessary runtime evaluation while loading the plugin
- The validation schemas themselves need a few changes to protect against ValueErrors being raised, and some simplifications should be applied. I have a local diff here, but I'm not going to post that now.
- The PluginError should not be raised for offline streams and instead `None` should be returned. The error message should be logged instead via `log.error()`
- The plugin constructor is unnecessary and the channel attribute can be a simple variable in the `_get_streams()` method. The user-agent and referer headers are unneeded as well

However, as long as the site is still considered beta, we're not going to merge a plugin for it into master. Please see the contribution guidelines:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#plugin-requests

Adding a plugin for the 100th Twitch clone without any (probably actual) viewers is also already questionable. But since this site appears to be quite new, this can't be judged yet.

----

Regarding your DASH stream question, what the site is returning as a DASH manifest is a custom JSON based alternative, so custom stream-, worker- and writer-classes would need to be implemented for that.

>    If you have any idea how to directly reuse the added code in the existing find_files(pageId=...) selector (so there will be no duplication), let me know.

The hard part about integrating the added code to `find_files` is that `find_files` is cluttered with many different cases. Almost all parameters can be either a string or a pattern. IMO, we should split that method into smaller methods for the separate search cases.

Consider this method invocation: `find_files(fileGrp='DEFAULT')`. Although we just want to get all files that belong to that file group without other search parameters, we must still iterate over all file elements of the file group to check the other search parameters even when they are not presented. So the invocation above still has a linear execution time instead of a constant time where we could just return the cached list.

If we run into issues with wedged containers, a pattern that I've seen work well is having the process touch a file periodically (to update its timestamp) as a heartbeat, and have the liveness probe just run a command in the container to read the age of the file.  This can be extended by adding conditions to updating the heartbeat, such as checking non-shared dependencies.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [x] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [x] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [x] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [x] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Hi @jcbedoyam. Thanks for this contribution! You were very thorough in implementing your intended functionality and integrating it with cvxpy. However, I think if we have  a "perspective of Huber function" atom then we should be able to have the "perspective parameter" ``t`` be a non-constant expression. If we restrict ``t`` to be constant then we can trivially produce perspective transforms using code like the following.
```
def perspective(atom, var_args, t):
    # var_args should be a list with positional arguments suitable for calling ``atom(*var_args)``
    t = cp.Expression.cast_to_const(t)
    assert t.is_constant()
    assert t.size == 1
    if isinstance(t, cp.Parameter):
        assert t.is_pos()
    else:
        assert t.value > 0
    scaled_var_args = [arg / t for arg in var_args]
    expr = t*atom(*scaled_var_args)
    return expr
```
just a few comments as I was reading

The errors were quite rare—they only happened three or four times since the first report on July 6 (#320), which means hundreds of CI runs. Perhaps they could be induced on a Mac by re-running that test in a tight loop, but then again, maybe that sets up the memory in such a way that new allocations hit a lucky spot of memory 100% of the time.

I created a branch (not a PR!) to try to make the bug more likely:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/compare/master...jpivarski/intermittent_error_bugfix-DEBUG

It is relative to master, so the bug should still be there. (I didn't want to take the chance that your PR fixed it, because then we'd be chasing nothing!) This assigns `-999` to all elements of all Indexes and integers that are to be filled before filling them in `argsort_next` with the idea that some value might be left unfilled and we're looking at uninitialized memory. That's probably not it because doing so didn't cause an error on Linux, and a value of `-999` ought to cause some problems. (I also tried switching them to positive `999`, in case it's something that's just compared with zero, like in IndexedOptionArray. That didn't trigger it either, on Linux.)

If you check out this branch and run it on MacOS, does it make the bug show itself? (What about changing all the `-999` to `999`? Adding more or bigger `little_arrays` in `test_IndexedOffsetArray`? In Python 2 vs 3?) If none of that brings out the bug, then perhaps we should just apply your possible fix and keep an eye on it for the next few months.

---------------------------------------

There are a lot of changes here unrelated to the bug; they're C++ features from clang-tidy. Some of them explicitly define or delete special functions (copy/assignment constructors, etc.), some of them add `explicit` and `final` keywords, which I'm not familiar with, though I've just read up on them. (I'm not sure why we're setting `final` on Forms; the class hierarchies are very flat, most concrete classes directly inherit from a single abstract class. Unintended subclassing is not something we're likely to do.) From the documentation, these seem to be exclusively C++11 features, which is required, though I don't know if CI would catch a C++14 feature, if one were accidentally used.

I've written inline comments about initializing class members to `0`. I don't know if that's related to the clang-tidy or if that was intended to fix the bug. Either way, I'd rather not do that because it gives the impression that constructors don't have to assign them explicitly. Everything here should be 100% RAII, with all class members set by every constructor. If there's a way to enforce it with a C++11 feature, I'd be up for that.

I came across another general issue. We currently filter out all unplayable tracks and this means we'll be removing them whenever we make any playlist change, which is wrong. Tracks might be currently unplayable if unavailable in the geo region where the user is logged in (but may be playable from elsewhere and/or in the future). I think local files might also be treated as unplayable but I'm not sure if Spotify still supports this feature I never used it. I think to workaround this we'll need to change the current playlist behaviour (which has been the same since our first implementation) to now include unplayable tracks in playlists. libspotify won't be able to load these tracks when it comes to try and play them and they will be skipped with an `info` log message (which we will want to adjust). These unplayable tracks will clutter the queue/playlist listing and might be confusing to users when they try to play them. But I don't see another way to avoid losing them when we use `save()`.

I've not looked closely at the myers implementation here, I assume it's the normal one that's available everywhere. It seems odd that in 2020 there is no standard, fast (C), robust implementation available for us to just pull in and use. It's not ideal we have to reproduce (and test) our own version of this common algorithm. But we do have Python's standard `difflib.SequenceMatcher`, so what does using myers provide over just using that? I did find multiple (why???) packages for Google's [diff-match-patch](https://github.com/google/diff-match-patch) library. That seems to use many of the optimisations we originally talked about in #236 but I've no great interest in using that, seems complicated. 

At a higher level, some of what has been added to playlist.py belongs in web.py. You'll notice there isn't currently any Spotify Web API specific stuff in playlist.py. I think we want to keep that separation.

My main question is that we will now have two properties with a very similar content, which seems wasteful. We will never check these for validity.

Instead, my suggestion would be the following:

1. We only store the relative logdir path (let's call it `relative_logdir`)
2. `Trial.logdir` becomes a [cached property](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.cached_property) (use [this]{https://pypi.org/project/cached-property/) for python 3.7 compatibility, similar to dataclasses for 3.6)
3. For backwards compatibility, we should extract the relative path when `self.logdir` is set (`@property.setter`), but print a deprecation warning
4. Same for restoring pickled objects (if `state["logdir"]` is set, just call `self.logdir = state.pop("logdir")`

We should add (very short and simple) unit tests to show that these conversions work.

It would also be great to see a test where we move an experiment analysis directory and recover the same information, showing that relative path lookup works.

Let me know if you have bandwidth to add this, otherwise I'm happy to chime in!

This is a bad idea. Everyones code that is already using this library is expecting a None response if something goes wrong. This means there will be a significant impact if we starting returning a truthy non-None response on failure.

I would suggest this would be better solved by either:

I really like the idea, but I think some changes are needed (in addition to the minor ones in the comments below this one):
1. If a history-tracked model happens to already have a `change_reason` field, this code will cause problems.. I guess this can be solved by either:
    1. dynamically detecting this and making sure that this code's `change_reason` field has a non-colliding name
    2. renaming this code's `change_reason` to e.g. `history_change_reason` (since it's already [listed as a reserved field name](https://django-simple-history.readthedocs.io/en/latest/common_issues.html#reserved-field-names))
2. A subclass of `SimpleHistoryAdmin` that overrides the `form` field will have a `FieldError` raised after `get_fieldsets()` adds the `change_reason` field, which means that multiple existing codebases will likely break. This should be solved by either detecting it in the code, or by documenting it - see the point below.
3. All of this should be documented so that users know that the `change_reason` form field exists and is added by default. Instructions on how to disable/override the behavior should also be documented - and potentially implemented.
4. It would be nice to have a test checking that the `change_reason` field is correctly set when *reverting* an object to a previous history record (since the `change_reason` field appears in the revert form as well).
   Some tests for the problem scenarios mentioned in the points above would also be nice 🙂

Lastly, thank you for taking the initiative to implement this! 😊

The PyMeasure instruments section provides a number of features that make programming and maintaining an instrument easier. I've remarked upon a few in the file, but do also reccomend you to take a look at this part of the documentation: https://pymeasure.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/adding_instruments.html.

A lot of properties can converted into a `control`, `measurement`, or `setting` property. For those that cannot be converted into this, I would recommend using `self.write` in stead of `self.switch_data.write` and `self.values` (or in some cases `self.ask`) in stead of `self.switch_data.query` (though, if at all possible, it is always recomended to use the aforementioned properties).

Apart from that, you could also have a look at how the Keithley 2700 or 2750 is implemented, these are also matrix multiplexers and might be helpful to look at.

Finally, don't forget to add the instrument to __init__.py file in the Keysight folder, add the required documentation files in the docs/api/instruments/keysight folder, and add yourself to the author lists (one at AUTHORS.txt at the root of the repo and one in docs/about/authors.rst).

If you have any questions or anything is not (completely) clear, feel free to let me know!

Adding runtime-dependent behavior defaults is not the best idea: it should be consistent across envs. I don't think we should enable this by default but rather make it opt-in for as long as we support CPython versions below 3.8. If we were to have it on by default, it needs to be supported across all runtimes equally, otherwise, end-users would be confused as to what magic happens in some envs but not others, for example, by relying on a backport.

Also, this sort of change needs to be bundled with updates to tests and to docs.

Overall I like the direction and would love to be back in sync with the KeepAChangelog spec. I am a bit worried about a breaking change and how we support a migration. We removed a few command line options so that would be a breaking change. We could add those back in and throw a deprecation warning. 

We could do feature detection on if there is an existing changelog with the old style keep the old style, but that seems to add too much of a mess and makes migrating harder. 

We might be able to add a `cl migrate` or `cl upgrade` tool which converts from the old to the new in place and document that, and throw a command line warning asking users to use it if they have an old style file. In that vein, we could also alias the old commands to the new commands while throwing a deprecation warning, just to avoid the breaking change for now.

This is a fairly minimal change, and looks like a clean switch based on `sys.platform == "emscripten"`. (I don't know how general that is, though you've already talked about it with @agoose77.)

This only modifies the HTTPSource, and it does so internally, in a way that users can't inspect. However, the choice it's making is between a single background thread (CPython) and a non-thread (Pyodide), which are roughly equivalent, just a concession for Pyodide not supporting threads.

What about when the HTTP server doesn't support multi-part GET requests? In that case, HTTPSource falls back to MultithreadedHTTPSource (the `_fallback` attribute of HTTPSource). That launches $n$ workers to distribute the load of requesting a lot of byte ranges for TBaskets when an HTTP server can only respond to one contiguous byte range per request. Presumably, that would have to be serialized into one ResourceTrivialExecutor and become much slower, or maybe `async` could be used, but that's a more radical change to the MultithreadedSource, and should be a separate PR, if at all.

This happens automatically when an HTTP server doesn't support multi-part GET, but it can also be forced manually by setting the `http_handler` in `uproot.open`.

We're also not addressing local files, but I saw that you tried and had some troubles with this. As I understand from @agoose77, that part of Pyodide is in flux and maybe we should wait for it to settle down. I highly doubt that memory-mapping will work (the default), and the alternative is MultithreadedFileSource. The MultithreadedHTTPSource and MultithreadedFileSource (and MultithreadedXRootDSource) share a lot of code through a superclass, so converting it to use `async` or writing an alternative that uses `async` could happen in one place.

An `async` alternative to the multithreaded sources would be welcome in CPython as well. What the multithreaded sources are doing is essentially building async functions by hand (so it would work in Python 2). @nsmith- built an Uproot subset based entirely on Python 3 `async`, and if I remember right, it worked better in high-latency network environments. Oh, I just noticed that the Python [asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html) package is not available for Pyodide. That's odd—that's how I would have expected a JavaScript-like environment to work. (JavaScript is very asyncy!)

None of these things are holding back this PR. I'm just pointing out that if people are using this on Jupyter-Lite, there's a chance we'll see these other issues eventually.

- For AreaDefinition, many methods are not clear if they return data in xy or lat/lon; the pixel centroid or the outermost pixel corner. Maybe would be worth adding a common suffix (i.e. `_ll`) when they return lat/lons, and otherwise expect they return xy coords. This will enhance code readability. 
Example: 
   - `area_def.corners` currently returns the centroid lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   - `area_def.outer_boundary_corners` currently returns the corners lat/lon coords in the very old spherical object
   --> Possible new methods: `corners_centroids`, `corners`, `corners_centroids_ll`, `corners_ll`
- Add utility properties like `upper_left_corner_ll`, `upper_left_centroid_ll`, 
  A possible pattern: `<lower/upper>_<right/left>_<''/'ll'>`  (or bottom instead of lower).

- Add `xy_bbox `and `ll_bbox ` properties for consistency with satpy? Equivalent to `area_extent `and `area_extent_ll`
- Current `area_extent_<>` and `<>_bbox` follow the `(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax)` convention. More precisely the `area_extent` is defined as `(lower_left_x, lower_left_y, upper_right_x, upper_right_y)`.

A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like `get_keys_on_hsm()`, `get_hsms()` seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyKCS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable (optional if there are reasonable defaults for some systems)
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `hsm:device/0/slot/c9` or `hsm:?slot=c9&device=0`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
The remaining functionality would then be `export_pubkey()`. Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could?

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

Another high level idea after this change is .. i think with this change, we should revisit a number of references and variables passing around and try to bring them to the minimum set that satisfies what each modular component does. I'm afraid a few things are passed around and mixed together with our previous structure and won't make code easy to work with.

I'm thinking of:
1) Controller name -- do we really need to pass it around to each intermediate class's constructor ? We fetch controller namespace directly from serve.api's internal method and it should be a very simple global name for each serve cluster
2) kv store -- for each `BackendState`, i don't think we even need to pass this object anymore given it can be handled at `BackendStateManager` level ?
3) long_poll_host -- this is at least inconsistent with the behavior of kv store now, we're delegating all long_poll_host actions to per `BackendState` level, but doing the opposite for kv store. 
4) goal manager -- same as long polling host, we're creating it at manager level but not even using it, all actions happen underneath per deployment.
